data.title: Affirm: A Credit Business Disguised As A Payments Platform
data.description:
Thrust of my analysis

Affirm Holdings Inc. (AFRM) is widely perceived as a high-growth fintech company benefiting from the expanding Buy Now, Pay Later (BNPL) market. This view is largely driven by the double-digit revenue growth due to the rapid adoption of installment-based payments.

However, such narratives tend to overlook the business economics. In this article, I took a more contrarian approach by examining Affirm through the lens of capital intensity, unit economics, and competitive structure. I focused on how growth is funded, how profits are generated, and whether the company possesses durable competitive advantages.

The result is a more cautious and very different conclusion about Affirm’s long-term value creation potential.

Business background

Affirm is a fintech company operating a payments network that enables consumers to pay-over-time and merchants to increase conversion and average order value.

At the heart of the company’s business is its checkout-embedded pay-over-time platform. Because of this, many market participants categorize Affirm as a BNPL provider. However, the company itself positions the platform more broadly as a payments network enabling installment-based commerce.

This distinction is important when looking at the growth potential and the competitive pressures.

Currently the company derives its revenue from multiple sources – fees from the merchants, interest income from consumers, revenue from its cards, gains from sales of loans, and servicing income.

On average over the past 3 years, merchant revenue accounted for about 1/3 of total revenue. Interest income accounted for about another half.

At the same time, from 2020 to 2025, merchant revenue grew at 28 % CAGR, whereas interest income grew at 54 % CAGR.

The revenue mix points to a business whose growth and profitability are tied more to consumer credit rather than transaction routing. I would argue that from an economics perspective, Affirm is in the credit business first, not a payments network. A true payments network would derive the vast majority of revenue from transaction fees rather than interest income.

Secondly, despite its global ambition, this is still a US-centric business. Over the past 5 years, its non-US business contributed less than 5 % of the total revenue.

Note: Affirm has Jun as its financial year-end. Unless stated otherwise, the years in this article refer to the financial years.

Topline growth

From 2020 to 2025, Affirm’s revenue grew at 45 % CAGR. As I will show later, this is a credible growth compared to its peers.

Part of the growth was due to the strong market tailwinds, as exemplified by the following;

“The BNPL market in the U.S. experienced robust growth during 2021-2024, achieving a CAGR of 20.3%.” Fintech Futures

“From 2022 to 2023, the number of loans made by the (BNPL) lenders we surveyed increased by 23 %, and the total dollar amount of loans originated increased by 26 %...” US Consumer Financial Protection Bureau

Another part of the growth can be attributed to its acquisitions. While the company does not break down its annual revenue into that from acquisition vs organic, we can get a sense of this by looking at the amount spent on acquisitions vs CAPEX.

From 2020 to 2025, the company spent about $ 244 million in acquisitions vs $ 647 million on CAPEX. Of course, CAPEX in a year would include the impact of subsequent years' acquisitions. But ignoring this effect, a back-of-the-envelope analysis based on the ratio of the expenditure suggests that about 1/3 of the revenue growth can be attributed to acquisitions.

By any measure, a double-digit inferred organic growth is a credible performance. The question is whether this double-digit growth from the US is sustainable. This is where I start to pour cold water over the optimistic growth picture that many are projecting.

Narrow Moats

Sustained double-digit revenue growth would depend not only on sustained market tailwinds but also on a strong competitive edge.

In its various Forms 10k, the company has described its competitive advantages from its network effects, proprietary technology, data flywheels, capital markets expertise, and platform partnerships.

I think these are descriptions of why it can compete. But the company has not explained why competitors cannot replicate its platform features over time.

For example, Affirm uses technology and data science to evaluate credit risk and personalize financing options in real time. This tech-driven underwriting helps manage credit risk and supports scalability. But this is not a wide IP-protected moat, as similar capabilities can be developed by well-capitalized competitors.

Secondly, the loss of the Walmart relationship illustrates that merchant switching costs are not prohibitive. It shows that large customers can and do migrate to alternative providers when economics or strategic priorities change.

Furthermore, to benefit from the network effect, Affirm has to continue to scale its merchant and end-user base. In this respect, its long-term competitors include the large card networks, such as Visa and Mastercard, which already operate at vastly greater scale on both sides of the market.

Conceptually, Visa and Mastercard are already in the installment-payments ecosystem. They are probably playing in a higher margin segment. But there is no evidence to suggest that they are not potential entrants into Affirm’s target segment, should the economics become attractive.

My main point is that there are structural ceilings to Affirm’s long-term growth potential. I think that we can have a misleading picture of the growth prospects if we just assess the opportunity based solely on headline BNPL sector growth, without considering competitive dynamics.

Unit economics

The company only started to be profitable recently (Q1 of FYE 2026). The historical losses were because it was operating below its breakeven level.

Looking at Chart 1, you can see that to be profitable, the company needs to operate beyond its breakeven revenue. I estimated that based on the 2025 margins and cost structure, the company would need about $ 3.4 billion in revenue to break even.

The Q1 FYE 2026 revenue is equivalent to a full 2026 revenue of $ 3.7 billion. As such you should not be surprised by the profit turnaround.

Chart 1 illustrates that the profit turnaround did not come from margin expansion. Gross profit margin and the corresponding contribution margin post-2021 IPO has some slight declining trend.

Instead, profitability has been driven by operating leverage. Fixed costs have grown more slowly than revenue. This is reflected in the declining fixed-cost margin over the past three years. This cost structure, where fixed costs account for more than half of total cost, means that incremental revenue above breakeven has a disproportionately positive impact on profits.

Notes to Chart 1:

I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, R&D, Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

This unit economics picture supports my earlier concerns about its competitive advantages. The flat and slightly declining gross profit and contribution margin suggest a lack of pricing power. This is not a sign of a strong moat.

The current profitability has been driven primarily by topline growth and operating leverage rather than by improving unit economics. However, as discussed earlier, there are structural constraints on long-term growth arising from competitive pressure and the presence of much larger adjacent platform players.

Taken together, these factors imply that while Affirm can achieve periods of profitability at sufficient scale, its earnings power remains fragile and highly dependent on continued volume growth.

Returns

Given the breakeven picture, you should not be surprised to see that, except for 2025, the company did not generate any positive returns. Refer to Chart 2.

The chart shows that the returns seemed to start trending up from 2023, such that in 2025, there was a small positive ROE of 1.7 %, even though the ROIC was still negative.

My point about the negative and/or small positive return is that growth only creates value if the returns are greater than the cost of capital. Various sources have estimated the WACC to vary from 8% to 24%.

Until the company can get returns far in excess of 8%, there will not be value creation. Again, this is to point out that painting a picture of topline growth is not enough unless the company can deliver returns that exceed the cost of capital.

Reinvestment

Growth needs to be funded and one metric to assess this is reinvestment. However, for a fintech company like Affirm, reinvestment has to be defined differently than for manufacturing companies. This is because growth is primarily driven by increases in loan receivables rather than investment in fixed assets.

Accordingly, in Affirm’s case, I defined reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + net increase in Working Capital + net increase in loan origination.

The addition of the last term will reflect the true capital required to sustain growth. On such a basis, I estimated that from 2020 to 2025, Affirm incurred an average of $ 1 billion of reinvestment annually.

This $ 1 billion is about 62 % of the revenue over the same period.

Another perspective is to compare the reinvestment with the Gross Merchandise Value (GMV). From 2020 to 2025, GMV grew from about $ 4.6 billion to $ 36.7 billion, but required about $ 1 billion per year of reinvestment. This implied a high capital intensity relative to the transition facilitators.

My point is that over this period, there was a negative return on capital. In other words, Affirm’s growth consumed large amounts of capital without producing economic returns.

This picture that emerges is that incremental growth, as pursued historically, destroyed value. Moving forward, growth must reduce capital intensity or materially improve unit economics.

Peer analysis

I compared Affirm’s performance with the following peers that operate installment lending platforms. The goal is to identify the base rates (outside view) for the sector.

Block, Inc. (XYZ), Afterplay is now a subsidiary of Block, Inc., and pioneered interest-free “pay in 4” plans and remains a significant BNPL brand integrated into Block’s ecosystem. Its business often overlaps with Affirm’s core markets and consumer segments.

Klarna Group (KLAR) is a large Swedish fintech providing BNPL and fintech services globally. It has recently expanded partnerships (e.g., Walmart) that directly challenge Affirm’s U.S. footprint.

Sezzle Inc. (SEZL) is a U.S. and Canada-focused fintech offering interest-free installment payment plans to consumers. Like Affirm, Sezzle targets the core BNPL use case at checkout and vies for merchant placements and consumer adoption in the same markets.

Zip Co Ltd (ASX: ZIP) is an Australian BNPL provider. With expanding operations in the U.S. and growth in transaction volume, Zip directly competes with Affirm for merchant partnerships and BNPL usage.

As shown in Table 1, Affirm was a mid-sized company in terms of 2025 revenue and ranked No 3 in terms of revenue growth rate from 2020 to 2025. You can see that the sector is double-digits growth one.

Notes to Table 1:

a) Except for Affirm and Zip, the other panel companies have Dec as the FYE.

b) For those with Dec as the FYE, the 2025 performances were based on the 2025 Sep LTM results.

I compared Affirm's peer performance based on several metrics from 2020 to 2025, shown in Charts 3 to 5.

I found that while Affirm delivered comparable growth in a fast-expanding sector, it lagged on profitability and capital efficiency. Affirm consistently sat at or near the bottom of EBIT margins. Furthermore, it was among the subset of peers that failed to generate a positive average EBIT margin. Its unlevered free-cash-flow margins were also weak on average.

It suggests a company prioritizing scale and platform expansion in a competitive environment, but failed to sustain economic returns.

Its balance sheet is also relatively representative of the peers. Over the review period, Affirm generally occupied a mid-range position for debt capital ratios.

On a positive note, Affirm delivered the strongest EPS growth from 2020 to 2025 among the panel.

From a sector perspective, apart from a double-digit growth rate, most peers operate with meaningful but controlled leverage. There were very few operators that operated with genuinely conservative balance sheets.

The sector had weak capital efficiency, with multiple peers failing to earn acceptable returns over the full review period. Operating margins are under pressure, where negative or low-single-digit EBIT margins are common. Free cash flow generation is also generally weak.

The overall picture suggests a structurally competitive, capital-intensive industry. This is a sector where revenue expansion has consistently outpaced improvements in profitability, returns on capital, and cash flow quality.

Notes to Chart 3:

a) The overall panel revenue grew at 22 % CAGR from 2020 to 2025, suggesting that this is a high-growth sector.

b) Affirm generally occupied the middle position for the debt capital ratio over the review period. However, on an overall average basis over the review period, Affirn had the next highest debt capital ratio.

Notes to Chart 4:

a) Overall, Affirm ranked the worst in terms of the average return on capital over the review period. It occupied the bottom position for the past 3 years. Affirm was also among the 2 panel companies with a negative average return on capital over the review period.

b) Except for 2020, when it ranked next to bottom, Affirm occupied the bottom positions for the balance of the review period for the EBIT margin. Over the review period, Affirm was among the 3 panel companies with a negative average EBIT margin.

Notes to Chart 5:

a) Except for 2024, when it ranked the worst, Affirm occupied the middle position in terms of the unlevered free cash flow margin over the review period. But it was among those with less volatile free cash flow margins over the review period.

b) Affirm had the best EPS growth rate from 2020 to 2025. Note that for this comparison, we left out Klarna as it only went IPO in 2025.

The sector picture and Affirm’s peer performance suggests the moving forward, Affirm’s priority should not be revenue growth per se. Chasing GMV or merchant count for its own sake will likely worsen returns. The focus has to be on long-term value creation. This, in turn, will depend on disciplined capital allocation, margin improvements, and the willingness to sacrifice headline growth in exchange for sustainable returns.

Financial Position

I would have some concerns about its financial position, as its positive points were offset by the negative ones.

As of Dec 2025, it had $ 1.5 billion in cash, equivalent to 13 % of the total assets. But as mentioned, this was offset by having the next highest average debt capital ratio over the past 6 years (in a period where returns on capital were weak).

From 2020 to 2025, it only generated positive cash flow from operations for 3 out of the past 5 years. On a positive note, the positive years were from the past 3 years. During this period, it generated $ 830 million cash flow from operations compared to the cumulative loss after tax of $ 2.7 billion. While this is a good cash flow conversion ratio, it reflects improving working-capital dynamics rather than sustained profitability.

As shown in Table 2, it did not have a good capital-allocation track record from 2020–2025. Affirm funded growth primarily through external capital and balance-sheet expansion, without demonstrating value-creating returns. In the absence of value-creating returns, this raises concerns about the resilience of the financial position.

Notes to Table 2:

For a lender-platform such as Affirm, loan origination is a primary use of capital. Each incremental loan requires funding. This not only carries credit risk, but also competes with alternative uses of capital. Accordingly, loan origination is classified as a use of funds in this table.

Prospects

Based on the above fundamental analysis, I see the prospects of Affirm as follows.

a) While it has gone beyond breakeven, this is due to operating leverage rather than structural margin improvement. But operating leverage cuts both ways - if revenue growth slows, profits can quickly turn to losses.

b) The flat to slightly declining gross and contribution margins indicate limited pricing power. Earnings growth will likely depend on sustaining volume growth. But this is constrained by competition and capital intensity (reinvestment)

c) Capital availability will remain a constraint as growth continues to require substantial reinvestment.

d) Affirm has narrow competitive advantages that are execution-dependent. This creates challenges for topline growth and margin expansion.

The conclusion is that while Affirm appears capable of remaining a viable participant in the BNPL ecosystem, it does not have a dominant, wide-moat. Without improving pricing power, capital efficiency, or durable customer lock-in, it would be challenging to translate double-digit revenue growth into durable excess returns.

In simple terms, the company’s fundamentals suggest limited upside at this stage.

Valuation

Although Affirm is a fintech company, its growth is driven primarily by the expansion of loan receivables. Accordingly, free cash flow in this analysis is defined as EBIT after tax less economic reinvestment.

As mentioned earlier, I have defined reinvestment to include net loan origination in addition to traditional capital expenditure and working capital changes. This approach reflects the true capital required to sustain growth and provides a more appropriate basis for intrinsic valuation.

With this as the caveat and based on my view of the company’s prospects, I assumed the following in valuing Affirm.

a) The base revenue would be the 2025 revenue. Revenue growth would be 45 % in the first year, in line with the historical growth rate. But this quickly declines to the long-term GDP growth rate of 4% in the 6th year (terminal year)

b) The contribution margin and capital turnover would be the 2023 to 2025 average values and remain unchanged over the valuation period.

c) The base reinvestment margin would be the historical average of 62%. I assumed that the company would be able to improve its capital efficiency so that the reinvestment rate in the terminal year would be governed by the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment rate. The reinvestment rates in the intermediate years would reduce proportionately from the 62 % in Year 1 to the one given by the fundamental growth equation in the terminal year.

d) I assume the tax rate to be zero during the valuation period due to the tax loss/tax credits and 21% in the terminal year.

On such a basis, I obtained a negative value of equity for the company. This is not an investment opportunity.

Valuation model

I valued Affirm based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to Firm model as shown in Table 3.

Notes to Table 3:

a. Proportionate change

b. Pegged to growth rate

c. Assumed no change

d. Assumed long-term growth rate

e. Revenue X Margin and after accounting for Fixed costs. Zero tax rate during the review period

f. Assumed proportionate change

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at the terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the diluted number of shares

Risks and limitations

There are 2 issues to consider in my valuation of Affirm – the valuation model and the assumptions.

I have valued Affirm based on a Free Cash Flow to Firm model. Damodaran typically recommends using the Free Cash Flow to Equity model to value financial services firms. This is because "debt" for a lender is not just a source of capital - it is raw material.

However, I am not treating Affirm as a financial company. Rather by including loan origination as part of the reinvestment, I have turned into valuing Affirm as a non-financial company. While unconventional, I would argue that it is logically sound for a hybrid fintech.

The other issue is that the negative valuation is because of my assumptions. I would argue that my valuation framework is correct for Affirm, as it addresses 3 things that most valuation avoids.

a) I defined free cash flow economically as EBIT(1−t) – reinvestment. Loan growth is treated as reinvestment

b) I have aligned growth with capital requirements. Growth must be funded, and I do this by tying reinvestment to growth via the returns.

c) I forced consistency between unit economics, reinvestment, and returns. In other words, there is no “free growth.”

The negative values imply that the present value of future operating cash flows is less than the net invested capital. This happens because of the “pessimistic” interacting assumptions.

I have mentioned earlier that growth only creates value if the returns are greater than the cost of capital. This is very challenging based on the assumed 14.8 % average WACC as per Table 4.

If I assumed the best WACC of 7.5 %, all else being the same, the intrinsic value increases to $ 82 per share.

With the market price of $ 83 per share, this means that the market is expecting a longer growth runway or a lower risk business (given that the WACC reflects the riskiness of the business).

Note that in my valuation, I have assumed that the WACC remains the same throughout the valuation period. In theory, the high WACC today represents the high business risk for a company with a loss-making track record. As the company becomes profitable, I would expect the risk to reduce with a corresponding reduction in the WACC.

You could argue that I should be reducing the WACC progressively from 14.5 % in Year to be say 7.5 % in the terminal year. However, even if I adopt this approach, the intrinsic value is still negative. The issue is not just the discount rate. The issue is the high reinvestment rate.

Finally, in my valuation, I have assumed that the reinvestment rate would improve to follow that given by the fundamental growth equation in the terminal year. This would require the company to improve its capital efficiency. At this juncture, there is no track record of this. For example, there is no significant improving gross profitability from 2020 to 2025. Asset turnover over this period was also relatively flat.

Conclusion

My article is based on the view that Affirm’s business is fundamentally credit-driven rather than a capital-light payments platform.

While the company had incurred losses for many years since its IPO, it seemed to have turned a corner in Q1 FYE 2026. I postulated that while this was because it managed to go beyond its breakeven point, it reflects operating leverage and not margin expansion.

It suggests that profitability remains sensitive to revenue growth and cost discipline. This was supported by the unit economics analysis that suggested an absence of durable pricing power.

I also challenged the company’s view of its competitive advantage. I believe that Affirm’s competitive advantages are narrow and execution-dependent as they stem from technology, underwriting capability, and partnerships. These are replicable and do not create hard barriers to entry or customer lock-in.

The company’s economic value creation remains unproven, given the high reinvestment intensity combined with historically weak returns on capital.

Finally, when valued on an economic free-cash-flow basis that properly accounts for capital required to fund growth, I obtained a negative equity value. This suggests that the market’s expectations for Affirm are not supported by its current fundamentals.

Investment Thesis

Affirm is often viewed as a fintech payments platform benefiting from the growth of BNPL. However, at its core, the business is credit-driven. Growth depends on loan origination and balance-sheet deployment. At the same time, growth remains capital-intensive, and the company’s narrow moat makes sustained double-digit growth challenging.

While the company reported profits for Q1 2026, this reflects operating leverage and not structural improvement in pricing power or margins. Unit economic analysis shows flat to slightly declining margins. Thus, economic value creation remains unproven.

When valued on an economic free-cash-flow basis that properly accounts for reinvestment needs, I obtained a negative equity value. This is not an investment opportunity from a long-term value investment perspective.

data.title: nVent Electric: From Behind-The Scenes-Dull To Behind-The-Scenes Growing-And-Cool
data.description:
“Opportunity is missed by most people because it is dressed in overalls and looks like work.”

That quip from Thomas Edison reminds us how much basic grunt work is needed to accomplish great things.

Consider a movie or a theatrical presentation. None of the spectacular things we see on screen or stage could happen without the efforts of many carpenters, electricians, plumbers, seamstresses, tailors, etc. we never see.

The same holds true for our modern information age. We push buttons or speak to remote controls or intelligent appliances. We live lives through handheld mobile devices. Does anybody today still use wiring to plug in phones or get laptop internet?

But don’t underestimate how much old-fashioned copper wiring we still need and use.

An insightful study of Microsoft’ s impressive $500 million data centre facility located in Chicago revealed that it consumed an astonishing 2,177 tonnes of copper—equivalent to 27 tonnes of copper for every megawatt of power utilized.

This, the considerable and still mushrooming, use of wiring is the raison d'être for nVent Electric (NVT).

This company, spun out from Pentair (PNR) in 2018, doesn’t actually make wires. But it does make things wired systems need.

Wires need to be connected to one another. NVT is one among many outfits that make a wide variety of connectors for all sorts of configurations.

Wires and their connections also need protection from potential damage, from human actions or the elements. NVT is one among many outfits that make safety enclosures in which wiring can be housed to accomplish these purposes.

So clearly, NVT, as unheralded and unglamorous as it may be, is critically important to our modern electronics-oriented world.

It therefore looks like NVT has some elements of what former Fidelity Magellan Fund manager and noted investment guru presents as…

A Potentially Perfect Stock

Chapter 8 of Lynch’s classic One Up on Wall Street is titled "The Perfect Stock, What a Deal.”

The chapter discusses 13 traits that suggest perfection. Few, if any, stocks have all of them. But NVT has two traits important enough to have been discussed early in the chapter.

Number one consists of companies having dull or ridiculous names. I would not count NVT among those. But it does fit the runner-up trait, “It Does Something Dull”

Consider connectors and enclosures for wiring: Surely you can’t deny the sleep-inducing traits of those businesses!

That harkens back to the first part of the title to this article,“behind-the-scenes-dull.”

According to Chapter 8 (page 132):

A company that does boring things is almost as good as a company that has a boring name, and both together is terrific. Both together is guaranteed to keep the oxymorons away until finally the good news compels them to buy in, thus sending the stock price even higher. If a company with terrific earnings and a strong balance sheet also does dull things, it gives you a lot of time to purchase the stock at a discount. Then when it becomes trendy and overpriced, you can sell your shares to the trend-followers.

As we’ll see later, seemingly dull NVT does have terrific earnings.

(You might expect from the above, written many years ago, to see NVT trading at a shockingly low P/E, perhaps in the teens. That’s not the case. But as I’ll discuss below, the stock is a good relative value given the current state of the world and the market.)

Moving on…

Another perfect-stock trait is, “It’s a Spinoff.”

NVT came into existence exactly that way. And its existence since it became independent is as Lynch suggested (on page 133) it could be:

Large parent companies do not want to spin off divisions and then see those spinoffs get into trouble, because that would bring embarrassing publicity that would reflect back on the parents. Therefore, the spinoffs normally have strong balance sheets and are well-prepared to succeed as independent entities. And once these companies are granted their independence, the new management, free to run its own show, can cut costs and take creative measures that improve the near-term and long-term earnings.

PNR did, indeed, send NVT into the world with solid fundamentals.

According to aaii.com, NVT ended 2018 with a 5% return on assets (versus a 0.3% industry average), and a 41% liabilities to assets ratio (versus a 55.8% industry average). NVT’s return on equity was a tad lower, 7.1% versus 8.3% for the industry. But given NVT’ s lesser leverage, I‘m fine with its relative ROE. (More debt tends to boost ROE.)

That’s nice. But it reflects 2018. What has the company been doing lately?

As Lynch suggested might happen with a newly independent company, the answer is — Plenty!

When listening to NVT management, we frequently hear words like “transformation.”

Here’s a time line of the changes to NVT’s business portfolios since it became an independent company.

Now we’re transitioning to the second part of title to this article…

Behind-the-Scenes-Growing-and-Cool

Ever since going into the world on its own, NVT has made six acquisitions and one divestiture. This is what CEO Beth Wozniak referred to during the 10/31/25 earnings call as a…

portfolio transformation to (let NVT) become a more focused, higher-growth electrical connection and protection company (that) is delivering results and accelerating (its) growth.

Acquisitions alone could do that. But NVT isn’ t relying on just that. New products are also a big part of what’ s going on here.

Wozniak also said on the call that…

We are investing in new products, commercial capabilities and expanding 4 of our facilities to add capacity for data center and power utility growth.

* * * *

New products contributed over 5 points to sales growth year-to-date, and we have launched 66 new products so far this year.

* * * *

Looking at our key verticals. Infrastructure led the way with organic sales up over 40% with strength in both data centers and power utilities, Industrial and Commercial/Resi sales were each up low single digits.

* * * *

Looking ahead, we continue to expect infrastructure to have strong sales growth across both data centers and power utilities. We expect Industrial sales to grow low single digits and Commercial/Resi to be flattish for the year.

The foregoing remarks make it clear that not all of NVT’s oars are in the water at the same time. Commercial/Resi (i.e., residential) work isn’t brisk right now.

That’ s not unusual.

Few diversified companies enjoy big growth across the board. Investors can say the same about their portfolios — Diversification is regularly counseled to account for the reality that some things are strong while others are less so, and that we can’t always know in advance which will be which.

Bullish scenarios occur when the hottest part of a portfolio are also the largest. And that’s what’s happening with NVT.

Refer back to the portfolio transformation image shown above. Notice how the hot area, Infrastructure, grew from 12% of the business in 2017 (just before the spinoff) to an estimated 40%-plus by 2025.

And when NVT says infrastructure, it’s referring to two really big — huge — things: Data Centers and Electric Utilities, each accounting for about 40% of the Infrastructure business.

The sizzling growth in data center construction is the investment and business communities’ worst kept secret.

If anything, there are voices suggesting excessive growth, the possibility we may be building more data centers than we need.

At this point, I’ll refer you back to my 12/31/25 MYRG report.

That specialty-electrical contractor also serves the datacenter and utility markets.

I explain there the picks-and-shovels nature of MYRG’s business.

I would not say NVT is strictly speaking another picks and shovels play. It sells components that go into and stay in data centers. MYRG sells services in building them, that finish when the facility is operational.

But we can view NVT as picks-and-shovels-adjacent in the sense that its components are needed and purchased regardless of how profitable the facility ultimately turns out to be.

And, for the record, I’m not a data-center bear. I’ve been around long enough, too long according to my wife and son, to have seen plenty of technology evolution.

And if there’s one thing I’ve learned from experience and observation, it’s that we always crave and ultimately pay up for a lot more than many think, on day one, we’ll need — and we keep on paying because we still wind up demanding more.

Too, those who use AI for real work — not just as an excuse to fire customer service workers or to make pictures of a significant other that make them look like kittens — know how primitive the best of today’s AI really is.

Analogizing to the personal computer, I see us still in the equivalent of the Tandy Radio Shack TRS-80 era. We need way, way, way, way more than we have today and won’t likely profit much from AI until we get what we really need.

Beyond that, as I explain in my MYRG writeup, our electric utility infrastructure is OLD. It needs a ton of work, not just for new things like data centers, but also to simply let us keep doing what we’re already doing.

So all in all, I believe NVT’s Infrastructure business has a very, very long growth runway ahead of it. The prospect for eventual cyclical improvement in Commercial/Resi adds further appeal.

The latest quarter was very much on track. According to Wozniak on the earnings call:

Organic orders were up approximately 65% primarily driven by large orders for the AI data center buildout. Excluding data centers, organic orders grew high single digits. With the record orders growth, our backlog grew strong double digits sequentially.

Further, NVT is…

Doing Cool Stuff

Does that heading seem a bit corny to you?

If you do wince a bit, please do not blame me (or Thomas Edison, who I quoted above, or Peter Lynch). The line comes from the NVT’s ad agency (or PR firm, or some source like that).

It’s the tag line NVT is using to promote its liquid cooling business, which is very hot right now. (If this latter pun falls flat, you can blame me.)

Electronic activity generates heat.

This is not an earth-shattering revelation.

Chip manufacturers and producers of devices that use them have always worked hard to find ways to effectively dissipate heat. And as chips have become more complex in order to handle more stringent and complex requirements, the need to keep things cool has become more pressing and challenging.

Cooling traditionally was done by fans. Think of your own pc or laptop. Fans have gotten quieter over the years. But they’re still used — even in modern laptops.

But data centers are moving beyond fans. Liquid cooling is becoming the new standard. According to general contractor HITT:

From a data center construction standpoint, liquid cooling opens up a whole new world of possibilities. It lets us build denser server environments, reduce overall energy usage, and even scale data centers in ways that weren’ t possible before.

For clients, this technology isn’ t just about cooling— it’ s about staying competitive. Liquid cooling enables the high-performance computing needed for AI workloads, cloud services, and tomorrow’s tech—today. By providing a consistent, cold temperature directly to the chip, clients can stack more servers per rack, overclock their graphics processing units (GPU), and push their hardware to higher limits. Benefits include:

* Higher Efficiency: Liquid cooling pulls heat away faster and more directly, which means less energy is needed to keep servers cool. Data centers using liquid cooling often have lower energy bills and a better environmental footprint.

* Supports More Powerful Tech: AI, 3D modeling, and advanced simulations need massive computing power—and that means more heat. Liquid cooling allows data centers to pack more processing power into smaller spaces without overheating.

* Smaller, Quieter Spaces: Since fans and large air ducts aren’ t as necessary, liquid-cooled systems are often quieter and more compact. This opens the door to placing data centers in non-traditional buildings with the right supporting infrastructure.

(Emphasis in original.)

NVT entered this business in 2020. And it's been growing like gangbusters.

Today, there are many competitors in this arena.

Indeed, Super Micro Computers (SMCI), a controversial but generally well regarded stock/company among Seeking Alpha analysts (excluding me) talks a lot about its own liquid cooling efforts.

But the leader here is not SMCI. It’ s Vertiv (VRT). CoolIT and NVT round out the dominant trio.

And within this market, NVT has carved out an especially appealing feature — modularity.

On the 10/31/25 earnings call, Wozniak elaborated:

We are a leader in liquid cooling with over a decade of experience and more than 1 gigawatt of cooling deployed. Our strength lies in our ability to design modular, service-friendly, high-performance systems that simplify deployment and provide resiliency across large-scale environments. We differentiate with deep application expertise, complete system designs, lab capability, rigorous testing and a proven ability to manufacture at scale.

In September, we announced a new manufacturing facility in Minnesota, our second liquid cooling expansion in the last 2 years. This new facility is expected to begin production early next year and effectively double our overall footprint to support our record orders and backlog.

Recently, we were named to NVIDIA's partner network as a solution adviser with our cooling solution and design architecture. This brings both credibility and awareness with global customers designing next- generation AI facilities.

At the upcoming Supercomputing Conference, we will debut over 10 new products, including our newest generation of high-performance, high-reliability, modular liquid cooling solutions, purpose-built to meet the growing power and thermal demands of next-generation AI data centers. And we now have a new tagline for our liquid cooling solutions. We do cool stuff.

There you have it — proof that I’ m not the one who coined the “cool stuff” line.

The Numbers

Recent results were restrained by a divestiture (see above portfolio transformation image) and, like many others, tariffs.

But on the whole, it’s hard to complain about the company’ s quarterly revenue and EPS trends.

Here are the historical and forward growth rates.

(I compare NVT to medians among companies in the Electrical Components and Equipment industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

The negative EPS percent changes were impacted by a one-time change in certain valuation allowances. The other growth comparisons show NVT as being significantly better than industry peers.

Here’ s the breakdown by reporting segment,

Infrastructure-related business, oriented heavily toward Enclosures, is propelling the company now.

Here are the core fundamentals.

NVT makes adequate showings relative to industry medians. Its numbers here look better than I would have expected given its so-far short life span and its acquisitiveness. (With respect to the latter, new costs, especially capital costs, tend to surface earlier than is the case with new sales.)

It fares respectably, too, relative to the SPY medians. It falls most short of SPY in return on equity. It looks like that’s being caused by NVT’s lesser debt leverage. (Debt boosts ROE.) When it comes to return on assets, which is not influenced by debt, NVT handily tops the SPY median.

Here are the financial statement highlights.

We see the impact of tariffs in recent margins. But on the whole, we see a solid financial profile.

Risk

The clearest risk here is that the data center bears turn out to be right.

As discussed, NVT does have other irons in the fire. Most notably, there’ s likely high level of demand due to electrical infrastructure upgrade. And at some point, commercial and residential markets will pick up. Sill, a serious data center downturn would hit NVT shares.

And as with any frequent acquirer, there’s risk a purchase will turn operationally sour and/or financially disappointing. This hasn’t happened so far to NVT. But this sort of risk necessarily persist as long as NVT remains acquisitive.

And given the popularity of liquid cooling, there’s necessarily a risk a new disrupter will enter the field and cut into NVT’ s position.

What to do About NVT Stock

Here are NVT’s valuation metrics.

I’ve seen better. In particular, I saw better on 12/31/25 when I wrote up MYRG.

I don’t see NVT as being as undervalued as was the case with MYRG. But even so…

I do think NVT shares are better valued than much of what I’ve been seeing among stocks whose prospects are tied to AI or data centers.

That’s what brings NVT into the picture Peter Lynch paints regarding dull businesses and companies spun out from bigger entities… about being able to buy such shares before the crowd rushes in.

Here’ s the price chart.

NVT had a nice run over much of the past 12 months.

We see that reflected in the way the 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) led the 50-day EMA.

We also see it in Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO). Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

CMF and CO spent much of the past 12 months well into bullish territory. That reflected healthy motivation on the part of buyers. That, in turn, exerted upward pressure on the stock.

It’s different now. The discussed indicators cooled off as investors digest prior purchases.

In another sense, this could well be a pause that refreshes.

Business momentum, driven by strong infrastructure markets and new products, is brisk. So, those who don’t now own shares might appreciate an opportunity to buy in without having to cope with a rush from a FOMO (Fear Of Missing Out) crowd. (The PEG FWD, computed on what I think is a credible growth forecast, suggests the stock still has room to rise.)

And there’ s probably plenty yet to come considering NVT’ s tendencies and track record when it comes to acquisitions and new products.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy, ”Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see NVT as likely outperforming the broader market.

I translate that to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating NVT as a “Buy.”

data.title: The Trade Desk: A Falling Knife To Avoid In 2026 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The Trade Desk: Looking Increasingly Vulnerable in 2026

Looking back at my recent ratings on the stock of The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD), I couldn't help but question myself whether I had been too "unsuspecting" about its resilience to recover quickly from the stumbles that followed a series of poor executions and intensified competitive threats.

I'm having second thoughts right now because it didn't take too long for TTD to previously bottom out from such hammering, as the fundamental strength of the company has always been a rock-solid scaffolding in supporting its narrative as the demand-side platform leader of the so-called Open Internet.

In other words, the world outside the walled gardens should be The Trade Desk's realm to roam with fewer restraints and expeditiously, which is also manifested in its operating performance. At the height of its heyday in the past couple of years, it has consistently commanded and justified a huge premium over its advertising peers (in which it's classified), as the Open Internet still appears to be thriving even under the stranglehold and competitive threats from Google (GOOGL) (GOOG), which was ultimately ruled as an illegal monopolist, not once, but twice, for its search and ad tech case.

However, Google's incredible survival instincts and reemergence from the ashes have also been inextricably linked to the AI transformation that has also upended search as we know it, with AI models featuring ever more prominently in today's context. Hence, I guess some could contend that Google "fortuitously" escaped relatively unharmed, as the behavioral remedies applied by the courts thus far ultimately aren't assessed to be too inimical to Google's fortunes. In contrast, it has afforded Google the monumental opportunity to consolidate its prowess while leveraging its immense tech stack and distribution advantage to not just catch up with OpenAI (OPENAI) in terms of its most cutting-edge AI models but also close in on the MAU leadership that has helped define OpenAI's increasingly valuable business model (reportedly looking to raise funds at a $750B valuation).

For The Trade Desk, I believe the ramifications from these revelations show that the market could be getting more edgy about the long-term prospects of the Open Internet, as traffic appears to be curtailed further with the advent and proliferation of AI. Under the current circumstances, while it may not quite impact the current business model of The Trade Desk too significantly yet, Google appears to also be paying less attention to its less influential Google Network business and rechanneling its focus to building up its own ecosystem. In other words, it does appear that big tech has gained clout in their bid to reshape how digital advertising will pan out in the new era, while the AI model makers and their Magnificent Seven counterparts figure out how they are going to monetize e-commerce and ads within their AI chatbot interface.

TTD: Still Fundamentally Strong, but Cracks Could Stutter Growth and Margins

In my previous TTD write-up, I commented that while the competitive threats appear to be affecting its performance (the market has gotten it on point), I thought the pessimism was taken way out of context. After all, isn't TTD still the Open Internet leader for advertising technology? And it's not like analysts are projecting a steep decline in its earnings outlook (see chart above), because that clearly isn't the case. However, the languid response in the stock since then has also spurred me to reassess and contemplate whether the narrative is still relevant and whether any adjustments are necessary.

As the so-called champion of the Open Internet while acting for advertisers and media buyers, the path to making incremental profits from a shrinking traffic pool could be getting increasingly more onerous. That said, TTD's revenue base represents a rather insignificant market share of the overall digital ads market (estimated at 2% according to Morningstar), suggesting that the monolithic size of TAM should still afford substantive opportunities for the company to monetize.

Obviously, The Trade Desk hasn't been standing still, with initiatives such as OpenPath (helping to improve the linkages between the buyers and sellers better) seeming to be making inroads and opening up a crucial growth optionality when the core business is perceived to be facing roadblocks to regain the same level of gangbuster growth. However, questions about the transparency and clarity of TTD's current operating principles have reportedly spooked some of its partners and customers, although it remains too nascent for us to ascertain whether there could be a more marked impact on its P&L from 2026. Nevertheless, I believe it's an area of concern that requires investors to pay closer attention to this year, as the market continues to place TTD in the penalty box while reassessing whether this could be a year of growth inflection to validate a much-needed valuation re-rating on its hammered stock.

Unfortunately, such a narrative may not yet gain traction credibly, as Amazon (AMZN) seeks to continue its recent momentum and gains to carve out The Trade Desk's market share in the Open Internet, including inventory that's typically outside of Amazon's proprietary purview. Its close partnerships with the most significant media buyers in the market could also have unanticipated effects on TTD, given the cloud and scale that Amazon's advertising business is currently operating in (revenue of almost $65B in the past 12 months, which easily outperforms TTD's trailing 12 months revenue of $2.8B). I don't think there's a shred of secrecy or mystique in Amazon's strategic blueprint for 2026, as noted in this recent write-up discussing AMZN's recent advertising forays:

Heading into the year, Moss outlined a few top-of-mind priorities for Amazon Ads, including focusing on partners such as agencies and tech providers as priority customers to “reinvent” how they collectively grow their businesses. Meanwhile, the company is also prioritizing how Amazon DSP and its adtech can help connect across the open internet, expanding AI capabilities, and moving beyond traditional channels to reach more non-endemic advertisers in 2026. - Adweek article on Amazon advertising focus in early 2026

I think TTD needs to pay close attention to Amazon's determination to gain share in DSP and not simply dismiss the threat from the e-commerce behemoth. It's clear that the team from Seattle is looking to expand further into what used to be the bastion of The Trade Desk. What's even more challenging is that TTD's high rate take (assessed to be about 20%) is expected to remain front and center as fodder for Amazon to slice apart and compel TTD to compete even harder on the price/value ratio, as compared to the increasingly robust ad technology stack and the partnerships that are being formed by AMZN, potentially at TTD's expense.

I believe until TTD can resolve these issues convincingly (while also taking the AMZN threat more seriously and less dismissively, like a Code Red, maybe?), the market might confer a more constructive view that TTD management is determined to move into crisis mode to tackle the challenges head-on and avoid being steamrolled over by the Andy Jassy-led company in 2026.

Buy or Sell TTD Stock?

For a stock to have retested its November 2022 lows while the overall market has surged to new highs, that disconnect is deeply disconcerting. As depicted in the above chart, the dip buying has failed to stem further downside, with selling pressure unrelenting before the recent retest occurred.

I've consistently favored contrarian opportunities in fundamentally good stocks, and I do still consider TTD as one of the more prominent plays in the ad tech basket. However, I'm also reminded that if the market is banking on a structural deceleration in TTD's high-growth profile while it continues to face pricing and heightened competitive pressures against its big tech peers, compounded by the fears of a bigger consolidation in ad media buying, then the risks on its P&L could have elevated markedly to a large extent. And that level of uncertainty could cause its forward earnings multiples of 19x to compress further, closer to the ad industry multiples of 10.4x.

Hence, while I still think that TTD's oversold bottom could see at least a short-term boost, I'm less inclined to maintain my bullish rating, as I think the fallout of TTD's weak performance isn't assessed to be cyclical but could have deeper structural impediments.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: December Job Report: 2 Cuts, Not Yet
data.description:
Initial Enthusiasm, Then a Recovery

The S&P 500 Index (SPX) started to rally quickly at the time of the report, then dropped just as fast. The bond market saw a much stronger reaction with a very aggressive liquidation of Treasury securities (especially short-term securities like the 2Y), which resulted in the increase of yields at all maturities. The two-year Treasury yield (US2Y) jumped to 3.54%, before returning to the 3.5% range, as shown in the chart below.

Ultimately, investors scaled back their bets on rate cuts this year. Even though the rates were "repriced" in this way, the overall trend of the interest rates did not switch and investors are still persuaded that the Fed will lower rates this year, in spite of the Fed's more conservative stance than anticipated in December. Two full rate cuts in 2026 are still on the table, with the first one probably happening around mid-year.

Report Details: My Interpretation of the Data

My interpretation of this report is not whether the information contained within is good, bad, etc., but it is that the report is simply exhausted (or "tired"). The dynamics presented by the report are those of a job market that no longer creates large numbers of jobs, however, at the same time is not yet shedding unemployment. The overall negative three month moving average of -22,000 and the annual total of approximately 584,000 net new jobs created in 2025 indicates to me that the employment is in a late-stage phase, rather than still expanding, although the job market has yet to enter into a period of contraction.

Regarding the decrease in the unemployment rate, to me this is the most worrying part of the report. While a declining unemployment rate may be viewed as a positive indicator (i.e., a strong job market), I do not interpret this as such. Instead, I view this as an ambiguous indicator, due to the fact that this decline is occurring simultaneously with a reduction in the size of the workforce. In essence, part of the improvement in the data comes not from new hires, but from the fact that some people are stopping looking for work. This makes the number "more attractive" statistically, but less reassuring economically.

I also find the report underestimates the issue of the quality of job creation. When reviewing the various sectors of the job market, I see numerous industries experiencing contractions, including manufacturing, construction, retail, transportation, warehousing and temporary work. This is not merely a snapshot of an individual industry or a single event, it is a broader trend. Health care remains somewhat insulated from the rest of the economy, but even here, the pace of hiring is beginning to slow. This suggests to me that the job market is remaining afloat due to its own inertia rather than any inherent momentum.

Finally, I firmly believe that the markets are slowly coming to realize that the Federal Reserve cannot rely solely on monthly payroll figures to guide monetary policy decisions. I continue to envision a future that includes additional interest-rate reductions. Not immediately, but ultimately unavoidable unless this current trend changes. I do not perceive this report as changing the overall macro-economic outlook, but rather confirming it.

Why this employment data matters more than usual

I think there is one key concept which could be misinterpreted when analyzing employment data. So, rather than asking "how many jobs have been created", we really need to ask whether the U.S. labor market has stabilized or whether we're just hiding a slow-down in a set of numbers that, taken alone, tell little.

For this reason, this December report matters more than previous ones precisely because, first of all, it comes on the heels of a difficult stretch of time. Second, as I mentioned regarding the CPI, both October and November were distorted by shutdowns, incomplete data collection, and a host of temporary factors that affected the clarity of the numbers in those two reports. As I mentioned previously, I described that number as "Too Low To Be True," so I would not be surprised to see revisions to that number in the upcoming CPI report. Likewise, I'm concerned that the Labor Market may experience a similar issue. While it is true that the data from December will likely provide a clearer picture of what has happened, it is by no means definitive. More readable, yes. Perfect, probably not. It certainly would not surprise me if these numbers too are revised in future reports.

The Immigration "Problem"

Obviously, the context in which these data come out has changed; many of the effects of these changes, as well as many of the policy decisions made, have been felt in. In recent times, in fact, the U.S. labor market operated similarly in the following manner: every month, many new workers entered the labor force looking for work. Therefore, the economy had to (and again I want to stress, "HAD TO") generate many new jobs each month simply to avoid a downward trend, or to at least remain stagnant, and thus to avoid an increase in unemployment. If hiring had slowed, the system would have immediately become strained. Today, this dynamic does not exist anymore. There are fewer people entering the labor force each month than previously, mainly because immigration has decreased, also due to policies of the Trump Administration. As a result, the economy does not need to produce as many new jobs to maintain an equilibrium of unemployment. Essentially, employment levels that were considered weak a few years ago could now be enough to maintain equilibrium.

I know it is a difficult concept to understand (because it's very convoluted even in its explanation), however, it is very important to realize how to interpret the report today. Not because the labor market is currently strong, but because the pressure on the supply side has lessened. That's why payroll breakeven has fallen (I'll show it in the chart later). The minimum threshold of new jobs needed to avoid a worsening labor market is lower than in the past.

The primary effects

A number of new jobs which just a short time ago would have been seen as being "weaker" or as being of concern, today can be viewed as consistent with a stable labor market. Stable, but not strong. Rather, a stable system where, at least for now, is not breaking down. By this, I mean is that the labor market today may appear "stable" even with modest numbers, not because it is strong, but because supply pressure has diminished, as I said before.

This is why I wanted to share a graph from Crédit Agricole, which I received in a note, and which I thought was quite effective in illustrating why job numbers today need to be viewed in a different context than in the past.

Let me explain:

  • the green and pink lines represent the 3 month rolling averages of total and private sector new hires.
  • the blue line represents the breakeven point, i.e., the average new hires needed to keep unemployment rates from increasing.

As you can see from the graph, the breakeven point has fallen dramatically over the last few years. In essence, the amount of employment needed to "stay ahead of the curve" to maintain a stable labor market is far less than previously. This is why today, numbers that in the past would have represented weak or concerning levels of hiring activity can be viewed as consistent with a stable labor market. Please take heed however, as the graph also indicates another important trend. New hiring activity is not accelerating, instead, it is merely remaining above the newly established breakeven point. Therefore, while today's labor market may be viewed as stable based upon modest levels of hiring activity, it is due to decreased pressure on the labor supply side, not due to strong hiring activity. As long as the lines remain relatively close together, the system will remain intact. If however, new hiring activity falls consistently below the breakeven point, the stability in today's labor market could disappear rapidly and perhaps faster than the numbers indicate.

The effects of the report could be obscured

Even so, the impact will likely be diminished, or even reversed, because of an additional macroeconomic event taking place currently. It is also rare for job report to become a secondary concern on the day of their release, however, such is the case today. The expected Supreme Court decision regarding tariff will likely dominate market focus. I have extensively discussed this subject in another article and described the potential outcomes and explained how, regardless of whether tariffs were restricted, the issue of protectionism is unlikely to cease. In essence, while legal limitations may eliminate one method of enforcement, the government will retain the ability to enforce similarly restrictive measures using alternative regulatory avenues. For this reason, rather than the precise outcome of the ruling, what matters to the markets is the lingering uncertainty, which could weigh on sentiment at least as much as today's jobs data.

My Conclusion

The US labor market isn't strong, but it's under less pressure today than in the past, and this is thanks to the issue we discussed earlier (immigration, etc.). The report certainly doesn't signal an imminent deterioration, but it doesn't provide any basis for discussing structural strength. As opposed to confirming the labor market's strength, this report shows the system now operates on a much smaller "margin of error." As long as job growth stays at or slightly above the "break-even point", the employment cycle will continue to be in equilibrium. However, if job growth consistently falls short of that, fragility could emerge quickly.

That's why I believe the Labor Report does not represent a 'turning-point', but rather supports the fact we are entering a late-stage labor market - and the stability of the labor market is currently more dependent upon reduced supply side pressures than an increase in labor demand. This isn't necessarily a bearish sign, but it's also not a solid basis on which to build expectations of lasting growth.

data.title: Revolution Medicines: Pivotal Trial Readouts Coming, And Buyout Rumors Churning
data.description:
Top Line Summary and Update

I don't think you would know it to read the headlines of my coverage of Revolution Medicines, Inc. (RVMD), but I am a real optimist about this company's projects within the context of their clinical trials and data, particularly since we got our first real looks a year ago at what their KRAS-targeted therapies have to offer in one of the most devastating forms of cancer that we struggle with. My reservations about buying into the stock have hinged mainly on the fact that the market is extremely sanguine about RVMD, as well. And that, in my view, has created relatively small opportunities for upside, as one could imagine the company facing "reality" at some point as the pressures of actually selling enough to justify valuations exceeding $10 billion (now over $20 billion in market cap as I write this) are laid bare.

Over the past week, we've seen RVMD gain tremendously in valuation by the market, at times doubling the recent average price, on the basis of buyout rumors from big players. Today, I want to lay out my ongoing case for cautious optimism, particularly not allowing an investment thesis to hinge on these rumors. There is a lot to be excited for, but I'm still skeptical that a buyout is the most likely math to pay off here.

Pipeline Updates

Daraxonrasib

The crown jewel of the RVMD pipeline remains the pan-RAS inhibitor daraxonrasib, whose unique mechanism of action I've covered in previous articles on the company, as well as the highly promising activity we've observed in the setting of pancreatic cancer, where alterations in the RAS/MAPK signaling pathway are nearly ubiquitous (estimated 90% of cases have a KRAS mutation).

Since my last article in October, there have not been any data updates, including being a no-show at the 2026 ASCO GI meeting, which is ongoing as I write this. The company is present as a sponsor, but there are no new data to share.

Rather, for daraxonrasib, all eyes are turning to the upcoming results of a pair of randomized trials: RASolute 302 and RASolute 304. RASolute 302 is the trial that has completed enrollment, and it is taking patients with metastatic pancreatic cancer that has progressed on first-line chemotherapy. These patients will either receive standard therapy (usually a switch to a different chemotherapy regimen) or daraxonrasib, with two primary endpoints: progression-free survival and overall survival in the population of patients with KRAS mutations at the G12 position. PFS and OS in the overall study population are also key secondary endpoints.

RVMD has continued to affirm a 2026 readout of this phase 3 study, and it has various designations that would help it push through the FDA. Most notable among these is the Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher, which could shorten the time to approval to as little as 2 months.

RASolute 304, meanwhile, is a study evaluating the use of daraxonrasib in patients with pancreatic cancer that can be removed surgically; i.e., in patients who have a shot at curing the disease. Daraxonrasib is going to be given after surgery and any chemotherapy, comparing it against observation with the goal of improving disease-free survival. So-called "adjuvant" therapy is an important unmet need in pancreatic cancer, because even though the intent of surgery in this case is cure, most patients still do not have long-term survival.

Beyond these studies, RVMD is also still planning to initiate a frontline trial for daraxonrasib in advanced pancreatic cancer, RASolute 303, as well as a phase 3 trial in patients with previously treated non-small cell lung cancer called RASolve 301.

Zoldonrasib

It is also worth touching on the development of the KRAS G12D molecular glue agent zoldonrasib, which appeared to be quite active in G12D-mutated pancreatic cancer while improving dramatically on the tolerability profile compared with daraxonrasib. This agent was actually the basis for the most recent news release from RVMD. On January 8, the company announced Breakthrough Therapy designation for zoldonrasib in the setting of metastatic, KRAS G12D-positive, previously treated non-small cell lung cancer, opening yet another abbreviated road to approval.

KRAS G12D is a relatively common mutation among patients diagnosed with non-small cell lung cancer, and there are no agents available to exploit this mutation. So in my book this is very welcome news, and I very much look forward to seeing how this story continues to evolve.

Financial Updates

At the end of Q3 2025, RVMD had cash and equivalents of $217.4 million, as well as $1.7 billion in marketable securities. The operating loss was $315.3 million for the quarter, a near doubling year over year. After interest, the net loss was $305.2 million.

For the first 9 months of 2025, RVMD used $623.5 million in cash to fund operations. At this rate, they have an implied cash runway of approximately 4 years.

Strengths and Risks

RVMD is continuing full tilt with its pioneering RAS targeted treatments, so the hits keep coming, and to date we have not seen almost any setbacks with this clinical pipeline. So what's not to love, exactly?

Strength - A pipeline that continues to grow in power and spread, with potentially momentous catalysts on deck for 2026

The two main compounds in the RVMD pipeline are moving from pancreatic cancer to other solid tumors like lung cancer. Other drugs in the pipeline not elaborated on in this update are also exploring novel KRAS inhibition-based strategies and colorectal cancer and beyond. RVMD is positioning itself to tap into totally new markets that cover wide stretches of actionable genomic alterations in major solid tumors.

Couple that the first readout of a phase 3 trial for their pipeline, and there's a lot of reason to be excited. Talks from key opinion leaders in the space of pancreatic cancer therapy remark very positively on how quickly RASolute 302 was able to accrue, and right now there is very little skepticism among researchers about the prospects of daraxonrasib. In my view, it's one of the compounds very much justified to be added to that National Priority Review Voucher scheme announced last year.

Strength - There's obvious interest from bigger players, which could mean an imminent buyout

Anyone who has engaged me in the comments knows that I'm really not a fan of entertaining buyout rumors as part of the investment thesis. But it is unavoidable for the RVMD discussion right now, given that we've seen major media outlets reporting on not one, but two apparently "imminent" buyout deals from the likes of AbbVie (ABBV) (reportedly for $16 billion) and Merck (MRK) (reportedly for $32 billion). An AbbVie spokesperson has since denied being in talks to acquire RVMD, but the Wall Street Journal isn't exactly in the same nadir of credibility as r/wallstreetbets. There is clearly a lot of interest circling around RVMD.

Risk - Buyout rumors are ephemeral

That said, I can think of fewer paths to disappointment than going all in on a company with the hope of turning around a short-term gain through a buyout. If everyone knows it's coming, that's usually a pretty sure sign not to get overly excited. Take a look at the Janux Therapeutics saga to see how this hype cycle often plays out.

In the end, I can't deny that the interest is clearly there, but I'm not sure that the price tags being discussed are really on the table, given how much execution is priced into RVMD right now before they have sold a single pill.

Risk - Nothing is ever guaranteed, and pancreatic cancer has embarrassed many contenders

That said, if you pay attention long enough, you'll build a whole armamentarium of clear slam dunks that somehow manage to faceplant at the finish line. For me, history looks grimly on the likes of rindopepimut, magrolimab, IDO inhibitors, and more recently the disappointments of bemarituzumab and domvanalimab. And pancreatic cancer itself has filled the graveyard with promising therapeutic approaches. Doctors can't even rely on tissue-agnostic approvals in this disease. Consider, for example, that the pan-tumor approval of trastuzumab deruxtecan barely touches HER2-positive pancreatic cancer, with a response rate of just 12% in IHC 2+/3+ patients.

Past failure does not guarantee failure, of course, would be the optimistic counterpoint to the long-overused disclaimer. These KRAS inhibitors have shown real light in a very dark tunnel that has been pancreatic cancer. But it is not a done deal, and we have to respect that risk.

Risk - Competition is real here

Moreover, RVMD is not moving alone. We're seeing an explosion of alternative approaches to targeting RAS that is moving through clinical trials. RVMD stands poised to be first to market, but there is a chance that they do not end up being best in class. RAS is being quickly made druggable, and practically everyone is going to want to be in on that action, which could ultimately be a drag on market dominance for RVMD.

Bottom Line Summary

RVMD continues to be extraordinarily exciting to watch on the clinical front. However, the market's rabid enthusiasm for the company makes it very difficult to pin down an appropriate valuation at this time, considering the company has no sales. If it's successful, RAS has the chance to be a bigger overall market than a target like HER2, where a single disease state (metastatic breast cancer) alone commands a market size that is expected to grow above $3 billion by 2035. The opportunity is here, and we're poised to watch a real revolution. In the long term, if RVMD can continue to showcase good results, the upside potential remains massive.

But between now and the next time I would write about RVMD? There are so many speed bumps that could lead to a major correction. It is very hard to put a lot of chips on this investment thesis for the very near term. Something as simple as a buyout not materializing within the next few weeks could very well deflate the valuation back to the $70 range that the stock was trading at prior to these rumors.

And if a clear win fails to materialize on the regulatory or clinical front (i.e., if RASolute 302 does not show clear benefit over chemotherapy), then the downside potential is also massive. For these reasons, I continue to affirm my hold sentiment at these levels, with a stronger feeling that we're going to see a pretty steep correction. That's not an indictment of the company's underlying projects and potential, but more a criticism of the market's willingness to put so many chips behind a company that is still a ways off from proving itself completely.

data.title: December Jobs Report: Welcome Relief In The Unemployment Rate
data.description:
We are finally caught up with jobs reports at the BLS and back on a regular schedule, with the report this morning being our first "on time" report since September. The report comes as the labor market has been cooling for some time, with the unemployment rate up to 4.6% following the belated report we got last month.

I had good hopes going into this report, namely because we've been seeing a fall in initial unemployment claims in the gap between the last jobs report and our catch-up period. There's some seasonality at play in this data, but overall, we ended with claims around where we started the year.

BLS December Jobs Report

The headline of the report was a good one: the unemployment rate fell to 4.4% and the economy added 50,000 jobs. This was consistent with ADP Research (ADP) payroll data that showed an increase in jobs for the month, which is a good sign -- those two don't always corroborate each other.

The thing I was looking for most was what was going on with long-term unemployed folks, since I've been seeing the increase in people being unemployed for more than 27 weeks. This is the darkest spot on the report, where it showed that long-term unemployment had edged up month-to-month.

In better news, short-term unemployment fell alongside the 5-14 week group, which means that a significant portion of these people (almost 10%) got jobs. We know that they didn't fall out of the labor force, because those figures for people being employed part-time for economic reasons (i.e., "wants full-time work, can only get part-time work,") and for discouraged workers are falling at the same time.

When looking at the source of new unemployment in December, the only category that's positive is "new entrants." This is a good thing, although maybe not for the young people who largely make up that category, but it is the healthiest kind of unemployment to have. It makes sense in the "low-hire, low-fire" labor environment that we're in -- to see the growing group of unemployed people be those who didn't have a job before.

When looking at the establishment side of the report, I was mostly looking at where the jobs were being lost -- I already knew that the biggest gains would be in healthcare, as they are most months. Cue my usual tirade about this point; our economy is shedding blue collar jobs in construction and manufacturing, and all the new jobs are healthcare jobs. That is the hallmark of a maintenance economy. We can see it in the December jobs report too.

The other big loser, to no one's surprise, was retail trade.

Revisions Were a Pain Point Again

One of the things that doesn't get reported as often as it should when these kinds of reports come out is the revisions to past months, which can distort the data. October was revised down by 68,000 jobs, from -105K to -173K, and November was revised down by 8,000 jobs, from 64K to 56K.

That's a total reduction of 76,000 jobs from the last two months that were previously reported. Take that away from the jobs reported in December's report, and the three-month change is a loss of 26,000 jobs. Over 2025, the economy added 733K jobs, which was the weakest advance in recent memory, and is half of what was added in 2024.

The job market has been all over the place since April; another sign that much of this friction, and the reason for revisions, is businesses responding to shifting trade policy with the lowest-friction solution for them: reduce headcounts. Combine this with the introduction of AI coming for some white collar jobs, and you have a recipe for a labor market that is net-losing jobs over the latter half of the year.

If the healthcare and hospitality sectors weren't doing so much hiring, these figures would be disastrous.

There is no real cure for the revision issue other than time and less chaotic policy decisions coming out of Washington -- without judgment on the validity of the tariff issue, it should be clear that the way they were announced and how changes were made to the policy caused significant disruption to businesses. The new tariff policy is a web of 500+ announcements made since April, many of which are being challenged in the Supreme Court now.

If there is chaos inserted into your business, and your margins are suddenly threatened, it's an easy out to layoff workers. They are a business' stickiest expense more often than not.

This Doesn't Move the Fed Needle

The Fed meets at the end of the month to decide on rate policy. With inflation no longer trending up, the Fed has been eager to hold rates again and re-enter a "wait and see" period.

In my last update on the labor market, when the JOLTS report came out, I discussed this further:

The current Fed is also aware that whoever the next Fed chair is (my money is still on Governor Waller) is likely to be very dovish; that change-over will happen in May, which gives the Fed time to wait and see now before there is a renewed push in the FOMC for further cuts.

I expect us to be entering another period of "wait and see," at least until the June meeting, or April if we are lucky. It will depend on how future jobs reports come out and if we are close to a bottom in blue-collar job losses or if it gets worse. Fed funds futures, notoriously unreliable as they change quickly when new data comes out, are speculating that we will stay at the current rate this month.

Lo and behold, what was an 11% chance of a cut a few days ago when I wrote that article is now 5%. These Fed Funds Futures are fickle. A month ago, the prediction was that there was a 21% chance. Now dashed by this report's surprise lowering of the unemployment rate.

Conclusion

Ultimately, this December jobs report from the BLS was very welcome. The unemployment rate moderating is a good thing, and there is a hope that with decisions on trade policy slowing down, we've already passed the peak of unemployment for this cycle.
