That could be dashed by a rise in the January report next month, but until then, the Fed's path is clear: no cut in January, and fall into "wait and see" mode. I doubt we see another rate change until April at the earliest, June most likely. The labor market won't recover back below 4% anytime soon, but so long as it doesn't rise toward 5% again, that is a relief.

Of course, I am still disappointed in where the jobs are coming from, with healthcare and hospitality still dominating job creation. There needs to be more positive action in construction and manufacturing, something we haven't seen much of this year. I worry about the real economy being stagnant even as we add jobs.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Jabil: AI-Driven Revenue Acceleration
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of Jabil (JBL), we analyzed the company’s revenues and explained how its diversification has shielded it from major weakness in its Intelligent Infrastructure segment amid weak telecom capex spending, whereas Automotive and Healthcare were bright spots in its performance. Since then, the company recorded 3.81% revenue growth for FY2025 and was also supported by strength in its Intelligent Infrastructure segment from AI-related demand in data centers and networking. As management disclosed that AI-related revenue grew by 73.2% in FY2025, far higher than its total revenue, and in its latest earnings briefing raised its FY2026 growth guidance to around 34% YoY for AI-related revenues, we expect its total revenue growth to remain firm this year and believe it is well positioned to benefit from AI demand as a leading EMS company with massive scale.

AI-Related Revenue Rising as Share of Total Revenue with Robust Growth

We compiled Jabil’s revenue breakdown across its three reportable segments which are Regulated Industries, Intelligent Infrastructure, and Connected Living & Digital Commerce.

Based on the company’s FY2025 results, Intelligent Infrastructure was the only segment that recorded growth, rising 33.7% YoY to $12.3 billion, while Regulated Industries declined 3.3% to $11.9 billion and Connected Living & Digital Commerce fell 24.3% to $5.6 billion. Among these segments, Intelligent Infrastructure is the key and most directly tied to Jabil’s definition of AI-related revenue, which according to the company consists of three sub-segments including capital equipment (wafer fabrication to automated test equipment), networking (switching and system-level integration), and cloud and data center (servers optimized for AI). Jabil is an EMS manufacturer and plays a direct role in the AI infrastructure supply chain by producing systems and components used in AI, cloud, and high-performance computing deployments. In 2024, Jabil expanded into AI-optimized server manufacturing through a partnership to build OCP-compliant servers equipped with AMD and Nvidia chips for AI, ML, and HPC workloads, in collaboration with Axiado, which is co-developing servers with AI-driven hardware security modules. Beyond servers, Jabil also manufactures optical interconnect solutions after acquiring Intel’s silicon photonics transceiver business in 2023, enabling high-bandwidth networking between AI accelerators and switches. The company is also scaling its capabilities in liquid cooling and power distribution systems integrated into AI racks, positioning itself across multiple layers of the physical AI compute stack.

These are included under Jabil’s disclosed AI-related revenues, which grew 80% YoY in FY2025 to $9.0 billion, and management has guided it to grow 34.44% in FY2026 and be 36% of the company’s FY2026 revenue. Although AI revenue is only a portion of the Intelligent Infrastructure segment, its share within the segment has continued to expand, rising from 54.3% in FY2024 to 73.2% in FY2025, and is expected to reach 78.6% in FY2026 of the segment. Management’s AI growth guidance is also in line with the surge in data center capex that we highlighted in our latest Nvidia analysis, where we estimated global AI data center capex to grow 61.4% in CY2025, slightly below Jabil’s 80% growth rate, which may be explained by timing differences given Jabil’s fiscal year ends in August 2025. Management’s guided AI revenue growth of 34.44% in FY2026 is also consistent with our derived 21.9% CAGR forecast for data center capex through 2029, supported by sustained hyperscale investment to expand AI training and inference capacity.

In 2025, Jabil announced a $500 million multi-year investment to expand US manufacturing capacity for cloud and AI data center hardware, including server assembly, liquid cooling, and power systems. In its latest earnings briefing too, management emphasized “recent program wins” with a major cloud hyperscaler in Mexico as well as a contribution related to its recent Hanley Energy acquisition. We believe this reflects the company’s continued shift toward higher-value AI hardware programs as AI revenue becomes a larger share of its Intelligent Infrastructure segment. In parallel, Jabil entered the ODM server market with the launch of its J-422G white-box server targeted at hyperscale buyers. We highlighted in our recent SMCI analysis that ODM vendors such as Foxconn and Wistron benefit from lower-cost, direct supply models that bypass traditional OEM pricing, giving them an edge in AI server adoption. We believe Jabil’s entry into ODM servers positions it to capture similar opportunities and support further growth in AI-related revenue.

We based our forecast for Jabil’s AI-related revenue for FY2026 based on management guidance which is a growth rate of 34.4%, and in 2026 onward based on our previously derived projection of global data center spending growth, which we estimated using cloud market expansion and capex intensity assumptions in our Nvidia analysis.

Other Segments Continue to Weigh on Company Growth

In the next section, we examine the rest of Jabil’s non-AI-related revenue by compiling its segment performance based on the recent FY2025 earnings release and management’s growth guidance for FY2026, as well as comparing it against the market forecast CAGR of the related end markets to evaluate its growth outlook.

Based on the table, we broke down the company’s reported Regulated Industries and Connected Living & Digital Commerce segments into their subsegments. Starting with Regulated Industries, the segment’s growth performance improved slightly in FY2025 but still declined overall, mainly weighed down by the Auto & Transportation subsegment which fell by 6.8% and is also guided to continue declining by 4.9% in FY2026 based on management guidance. In 2025, global automotive production volume is forecasted to decline by 1.6%, weighed down by Europe and North America, which together account for around 34% of global automotive output, amid tariff uncertainties throughout the year and expected to put pressure in 2026 too according to S&P Global, which we believe may be a factor contributing to the company’s weakness in its Auto & Transportation subsegment. The company, however, highlighted in its latest earnings call that long-term developments in the automotive market remain positive, citing new customer wins and a focus on technologies such as “software-defined vehicles, advanced driver assistance systems, compute, and other powertrain-agnostic solutions,” which management described as “all growth areas” for Jabil.

Healthcare & Packaging is guided to grow 3.7% in FY2026, showing an improving trend from 2024 and closer in line with the Healthcare Equipment market forecast CAGR of 4.9%. Meanwhile, the Renewable & Energy Infrastructure subsegment, although better than the -22.6% decline in 2024, is guided to grow 4.2% in FY2026 and align with the renewable energy market forecast CAGR of 5.5%. Management highlighted ongoing headwinds in renewable energy, citing tariffs and policy changes as key factors. In Q2 2025, US solar installed capacity fell 24% YoY amid rising costs and supply chain disruptions linked to tariff revisions, and in Q1 2025, around $8 billion worth of clean energy projects were canceled due to policy uncertainty. That said, long-term demand remains intact, with OPEC forecasting a 23% increase in global energy demand by 2045, while ExxonMobil projects a 70% rise in global electricity demand over the next 25 years amid the boom in AI infrastructure deployments.

For the Connected Living & Digital Commerce segment, performance has remained weak over the past two years, with FY2026 growth still guided to decline by 10.7%. This is due mainly to the continued weakening of the Connected Living subsegment, which is tied to consumer electronics and IoT devices. According to management, the company is “pruning lower-margin, short-lifecycle programs in legacy consumer electronics that don’t match the margin profile we desire,” indicating that the decline is mainly due to Jabil redirecting its focus away from legacy consumer products and toward higher-value areas such as data center servers. The Digital Commerce subsegment recovered in 2025 and is expected to grow 13.0% in FY2026, which is slightly higher than the industrial automation market forecast CAGR of 10.8%. Lastly, the Intelligent Infrastructure segment (excluding AI-related revenue) declined 21.4% in FY2025 but is forecasted to be flat in FY2026.

We based our 2026 forecasts for each subsegment primarily on management’s growth guidance, and for 2027 onward, we applied the respective market forecast CAGR for each end market. For the Intelligent Infrastructure segment excluding AI, we assumed a conservative 0% growth rate as we expect the company to prioritize capacity and resources toward the AI-related portion of the segment rather than legacy infrastructure programs. For Connected Living, we applied its 3-year average growth rate beyond 2026, as the company is actively reducing exposure to “lower-margin, short-lifecycle” consumer electronics programs and reallocating capacity toward higher-margin areas. This results in continued contraction in Connected Living, partial recovery in Digital Commerce, and moderate growth in Regulated Industries in line with their respective market outlooks.

Competitiveness in EMS for AI-related Products

In this section, we examine whether Jabil has a competitive edge over other EMS competitors in the AI-related business. We compiled a comparison of EMS players and their AI capabilities to assess whether Jabil holds any differentiated advantage.

In our previous analysis of Jabil, we compared the company’s scale against EMS peers by examining the number of manufacturing sites, geographic footprint, and supplier base. Jabil ranked third overall, behind Foxconn (FXCOF) and Flex (FLEX), with 97 manufacturing sites across 28 regions and 38,000 suppliers, compared to Foxconn’s 233 sites, and Flex’s 122 sites and 16,000 suppliers. As we noted, “the number of manufacturing sites, geographic footprint, and number of suppliers an EMS company has can indicate its ability to mitigate localized disruptions, regional risks, and component shortages.” Based on the table, Jabil stood out not only due to its global scale but also because it had the largest supplier base among all competitors, almost double Flex and far above Wistron’s 2,152 suppliers and Celestica’s 4,000. We previously concluded that “these factors position Jabil competitively within the EMS market in terms of diversification in its operations,” and that the company’s continued expansion, including new site openings in Europe and India further highlights the company’s continued commitment to grow its scale.

We also compiled whether each EMS competitor is involved in AI-related manufacturing, specifically server assembly, and found that most leading EMS companies, except Plexus (PLXS) and Kimball (KE), are involved in AI server production and have partnerships with Nvidia (NVDA), which holds over 90% share of the data center GPU market due to its performance and CUDA software advantage. We believe this is due to the low-value-add nature of EMS, where manufacturing is limited to assembly rather than chip or component design, resulting in customers spreading orders across multiple EMS vendors to reduce dependency and negotiate lower pricing. We believe Jabil’s main differentiation remains its scale, where it ranks second only to Flex, rather than exclusive access to AI customers or technology.

We believe most EMS companies stand to benefit from the growth in AI hardware demand, supported by their partnerships with Nvidia and AMD as AI server volumes scale. However, we view Jabil’s main strength relative to peers as its superior scale, ranking second only to Flex in manufacturing sites, geographic footprint, and supplier depth. We believe scale is a critical factor in EMS because the business is low-margin, with Jabil’s gross margin at only 8.11% (10-year average) and larger volume supports greater economies of scale and may allow it to offer more competitive pricing for high-volume assembly. The company has committed $500 million in new capex to expand its AI-focused manufacturing capacity in the US, signaling its continued focus on AI servers.

Risk: Low Margins

We believe one of the key risks for the company is its low margins. Jabil’s 10-year average net margin is only 1.85%, which is just barely above 0%, and its profitability could be at risk if adverse events occur, such as a rise in component or labor costs, or increased competitive pricing pressure from other EMS vendors. A negative impact on its margins could risk its ability to fund its capex expansion plans to support the growth of its AI-related businesses.

Valuation

Overall, we compiled our revenue projections for Jabil based on the split between AI-related revenue and total revenue excluding AI. We see AI-related revenue growing much faster than non-AI revenue, with AI revenue rising 34.44% in 2026 offsetting weakness in the rest of the business (-2.4%), for a total growth rate of 8.72% in FY2026 compared to 3.18% in FY2025, and accelerate to 10.3% in the following year as its AI revenue share of total company revenue increases.

Based on a discount rate of 10.1% (company WACC) and a terminal value using the 5-year average EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.36x, we derive an upside of 17.2%.

Verdict

Jabil is undergoing a major shift from low-growth EMS end markets and legacy segments such as consumer electronics, renewables, and automotive into a beneficiary of the AI infrastructure build-out, as its Intelligent Infrastructure segment, which consists of servers and other AI-related components, is set to become its key growth driver, with AI-related revenue guided by management to continue growing by 34.4% in 2026, supporting its total growth. We believe Jabil’s immense scale, supplier depth, and global manufacturing footprint and expansions position it well to continue capturing AI growth opportunities, and we forecast its total revenue to grow at a forward average of 10.8%. Recently, the company has disclosed that it has completed the acquisition of Hanley Energy for a $725 million deal with additional consideration contingent on its performance, to which the market reacted negatively as its stock declined by 7% following the news but we see this deal as not unreasonable given the expected revenue contribution to Jabil is around $375 million in the midpoint, which is a sales multiple of only 1.9x, and this acquisition also expands Jabil’s capabilities in power distribution systems related to data centers which it is heavily trying to focus on for growth. We thus rate the company as a Buy with a higher price target of $261.70 (previously $215.58) with the main driver being the expected revenue growth lift due to its fast-growing AI-related revenues offsetting the weakness of its broader business segments.

data.title: What Happened In 2025? Plus A Few Ideas On How To Stickhandle Through 2026
data.description:
2025 might be broken into two parts. In the first quarter of 2025, the markets feared the consequences of a global tariff war. U.S. stocks fell by almost 21% in the first quarter. And then, the markets largely ignored tariffs as President Trump often backed down from his tariff threats. From early April, the market went on to deliver generous gains. For 2025, the U.S. market was up by 17.85% if we use iShares IVV as a benchmark. That follows years of 24.98% in 2024 and 26.27% in 2023. Analysts predict more gains in 2026. But here's the thing: just like me, they don't have a clue.

Let's start with market predictions. From one of my recent blog posts...

According to fresh Bloomberg data, the average year-end 2026 target for the S&P 500 among major investment strategists stands at 7,555. The range spans from a low of 7,000 to a high of 8,100 and implies upside of 9% from current levels.

And on the expert's track record...

Predicting warm, sunny weather before the blizzard

Paul Hickey, a founder of Bespoke Investment Group, has been compiling these predictions for years. He has found that since Dec. 31, 2000, the Wall Street consensus has only ever predicted annual gains—every single year.

The market didn’t rise every year, of course. In 2022, for example, the S&P 500 fell 19.4%. The consensus forecast called for a gain of 3.9%—a discrepancy amounting to 23.3 percentage points. All told, through 2024, the S&P 500 fell in seven of 25 calendar years, or 28% of the time. The losses have sometimes been gargantuan, like the 38.5% crash in 2008.

If you look at just the overall averages, you might not see how ludicrously bad the professional stock forecasters really are. The average annual forecast of price gains for this entire period was 8.9%, not too far from the actual average performance of 7.7% for the S&P 500 annually.

But because the consensus was positive in all of the down years, and because many of the good years were far better than the consensus predicted, the Wall Street forecasts were off by an average of 14.1 percentage points annually. In other words, on average, the error was more than 50% bigger than the forecast.

The mistakes were so large that they were like predicting warm, sunny weather before the arrival of a major blizzard.”

My 'suggestions' heading into 2025

If we back up just before the start of 2025, in December of 2024 I suggested that there might be value in the Canadian dogs.

That's our version of the dogs of the Dow. These Canadian huskies went on to deliver 29.53% in 2025. For American investors, you can tack on another 4.7% due to the outperformance of the Loonie vs. the U.S. dollar. As you likely know, the U.S. dollar fell by almost 10% in 2025 vs. a basket of global currencies.

The returns of the dogs, the Beat the TSX Portfolio, were mostly in line with the Canadian market. Surprisingly, the Canadian stock market was the top performer of the top ten largest stock markets on the planet. Who had that on their 2025 bingo card? Not many.

Ignore Trump

Heading into 2025, I suggested that you ignore who is in the White House -

Democrats' stock returns beat Republicans, but it still doesn't matter who wins.

The first bullet point for that post -

  • It really doesn't matter who is in the White House. The U.S. economy is a much more powerful force than the President.

And more on the theme of the post...

The returns for U.S. stocks are much greater under the Democrats. That said, the returns, historically, are quite good and very positive, no matter what party holds the big seat. The Biden administration owns the second-best performance record for stocks since 1945. The Trump administration holds down the number 3 slot. How do you shape your portfolio based on the next President of the United States? You don't.

A diversification idea that I put on the table (again) - gold makes the balanced portfolio better ...

Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) is on a massive run to all-time highs as the Trump promise of massive tariffs will likely be inflationary, and tax cuts will balloon the deficits. But for the record, neither party has an interest in tackling the runaway debt.

Maybe gold is the sensible investment idea here?

Yes, it turns out that gold was a sensible investment idea in 2025. Gold was up almost 70%, its strongest year since the late 70s during the stagflation era.

And yes, I offered some solid and staple ideas on how to manage the risks in 2025.

U.S. equities greatly underperformed

I simply suggested greater diversification beyond a U.S. home bias. A global portfolio outperformed the U.S. market in 2025. Here's the comparison in 2025, in U.S. dollars.

  • International equities (EFA) 31.38%
  • Canadian equities (EWC) 36.03%
  • U.S. equities (IVV) 17.85%

Add in that gold, and you were golden, alright.

And when things were tough in the first quarter, the idea of defensive stocks held up ...

And mostly what I suggested heading into 2025, and I'll repeat for 2026 -

Adopt a sensible investment plan and stick to it like glue. - Dale

Personally, I'm sticking to a global portfolio. Being in semi-retirement, I like to shade in some defensive stocks and defensive ETFs. I also hedge the valuation issues with U.S. stocks.

Are pricey U.S. stock valuations a threat to recent retirees?

Consider hedging pricey U.S. stocks

Our U.S. stock portfolio holds some growth but is largely tilted to quality and value. In Canadian accounts I also built up the iShares Core MSCI US Quality Dividend Index ETF (XDU:CA).

So the 'big idea' for retirees and those who want to manage risk is to continue to hold some U.S. market ETFs or stocks but layer in a value tilt and manage your overall U.S. equity weight.

While many forecasts have U.S. stocks delivering no returns over the next decade, Vanguard suggests muted returns in the area of 3.5% to 5.5% annually. Valuation matters in retirement. Accumulators with decades to go might ignore valuation and stay the course. You might have time to recover, and buying at any lower prices that show up is advantageous.

All bubbles burst. When? Once again, I don't have a clue.

Thanks for reading. We'll see you in the comment section. I do address all questions and queries. Happy New Year, and all the best in 2026 and beyond.

data.title: December Jobs Report: A Positive But Cooling Finish To 2025 Payrolls Calendar
data.description:
The start of the new calendar year is bringing more of the same to equity markets. That is, continued gains and new index milestones. The run-up in equities is despite rising geopolitical tension with regard to the U.S. military operation in Venezuela. Market indexes, at present, are mostly shrugging at the unfolding events. Investors are also likely to continue taking the slowing labor economy in stride as well, in my view.

On Friday, the Labor Department released its final jobs report of the 2025 calendar year, and the data largely landed within expectations, albeit lower than the top-line estimate. In the pre-market trading hours just prior to the release, all three indexes were quiet following several strong days of momentum.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) was tracking just below the 50,000 milestone, while the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq Composite (NDX) were also near record territory. In the bond markets, yields on long-term U.S. Treasuries (US10Y) were settling near the 4.2% mark.

Today’s job report provided further data evidence that the labor market is slowing, but not overly so as to cause alarm. The data, in my view, is unlikely to change the current trajectory of Federal Reserve (“Fed”) policy. Here’s all to know about the December jobs report.

What Sectors Added Jobs In December?

During the month of December, payroll growth changed little, according to the Labor Department's monthly release. Employers added 50,000 jobs during the month. While this was shy of expectations, I still view it as within the reasonable range of expectations, especially since the rate of growth is above the average monthly gain of 49,000 through the entirety of 2025.

Within individual categories, gains continued to be concentrated in the familiar categories of health care and social assistance. Health care added another 21,000 jobs in December. While positive, it's worth noting that the gains were below the monthly average of 34,000 jobs per month.

Growth in social assistance employment was primarily due to gains in family services, which added 13,000 out of the 17,000 jobs.

The gains in the above sector were offset by losses elsewhere, including in retail, which lost 25,000 jobs. This could be due to the timing of the holiday season, which included a lower number of shopping days.

On an overall basis, I view the overall additions as reflective of a slowing labor economy, but not too weak to cause alarm just yet.

Unemployment And Wage Growth Trend

The December jobs report also showed little change in the unemployment rate, with the headline figure landing at the 4.4% mark. In prior analysis, I’ve highlighted stability as the key theme with regard to the unemployment rate. This theme continued into December, in my view.

While the unemployment rate is no longer sitting at cycle lows, it continues to hold steady month to month, suggesting that labor market cooling is occurring through slower hiring rather than an acceleration in job losses. That distinction continues to matter, in my view.

This narrative was already foreshadowed by the JOLTS report released earlier in the week. Job openings declined in November, and hiring activity also stepped down, signaling softer labor demand. Importantly, however, layoffs did not rise. In fact, involuntary separations edged lower.

I believe this provides evidence that employers remain reluctant to cut headcount outright. The modest uptick in the quit rate also suggests workers still feel reasonably confident in their ability to change jobs, even as opportunities become more selective.

Wage growth followed a similar theme in December. Earnings continue to rise at a more measured pace, easing inflation pressures without undermining household income.

Fed Policy Implications

I don’t believe that today’s release of the December job report will materially change the near-term trajectory of Fed policy. With the labor market continuing to cool gradually, the data largely reinforces what policymakers signaled at the December FOMC meeting.

The current easing cycle has entered a more cautious, fine-tuning phase. I’ve written previously that recent rate cuts were framed as insurance against labor market weakness, not the beginning of an aggressive push lower, and nothing in today’s report meaningfully challenges my stance on that, in my view.

What I believe does loom larger for markets is the leadership transition at the Fed. President Trump has indicated that a new chair nomination could come later this month, and investors are increasingly positioning for the possibility of a less independent central bank, particularly in the back half of the year. While markets have not reacted sharply so far, the risk is that aggressive rate cuts amid still-firm growth could push long-term yields higher, undermining the intended easing.

The recently released FOMC minutes highlighted just how narrow the policy consensus has become. Several officials described December’s cut as a close call, while others openly favored holding rates steady. The minutes also revealed growing resistance to further easing unless inflation shows clearer progress toward the target.

For now, January still looks like a pause. But with leadership uncertainty rising and internal divisions deepening, I believe policy risk may increasingly come from how decisions are made, not just what the Fed ultimately does.

Final Thoughts On The December Jobs Report

In my view, December’s jobs report brings a familiar and, in many ways, reassuring close to the year. Employers added 50,000 jobs during the month, a figure that landed below topline expectations but still within a reasonable range.

Job gains also once again remained concentrated in familiar areas, particularly health care. I believe this shows that labor market strength continues to be sector-specific rather than broad-based. Importantly, nothing in the report suggests a sudden deterioration in labor conditions.

Taken together with the earlier JOLTS release, the data confirms a labor market that is cooling but doing so in an orderly fashion. Hiring has slowed, unemployment remains stable, and wage growth is easing at a pace that supports consumption without reigniting inflation pressures. That balance helps explain why markets have taken the report largely in stride, even as equities continue to trade near record levels and bond yields hover around recent ranges.

In my view, today’s data is unlikely to materially alter the trajectory of Fed policy or the market’s current direction. The Federal Reserve appears firmly in a wait-and-see posture, and this report does little to disrupt that framework.

Looking ahead, investor attention is likely to shift quickly toward earnings season. With labor conditions cooling, I believe it will be continued earnings resilience, rather than jobs data, that serves as the more meaningful catalyst for markets in the months ahead.

data.title: 2027: Defense Boom As The AI Trade Unwinds
data.description:
War is typically unpopular with voters, but nonetheless, defense spending has re-emerged as a vital element amid Cold War II. Putting ideology aside, the key objective issue is risk management, whereby state actors are operating under increasingly fragmented geopolitical conditions.

China, Iran, Russia, and others are viewed as long-term strategic competitors rather than static counterparts that can be relied upon cooperatively, ultimately shifting government spending toward defense-based deterrence and capacity rebuilding. NATO defense budgets are increasing, Taiwan is being transferred arms, and missile-defense systems are driven by leverage-centric, tail-risk scenarios.

Historical analyses of regime outcomes—like R. J. Rummel's estimate that between 1900–1987, communist regimes are estimated to have caused 110,286,000 deaths versus 2,028,000 deaths under democratic regimes that were predominantly capitalist—can be seen by some to justify a "deterrence through strength" framework. Whether you accept these interpretations or not, higher and more durable defense outlays across the U.S. and allied economies are the result. Defense demand is currently in a structural supercycle.

At the same time, AI capex is booming across the allied world—even while China tries to edge its way to second-order dominance in autonomous tech. With macro markets heavily overvalued, many feel like 2026 could be a repeat of 1999. AI capex will inevitably digest—multiple times—along this decades-long tsunami of autonomous technology:

What defense spending provides in 2027 is a higher equity market floor that will help to stabilize the AI-capex-induced macro correction.

Why Defense Spending Is Rising Now

The high-consumption war in Ukraine has reminded the world of the reality that what is excess in peace can become minimum viability in times of threat. Moreover, the industrial nature of shipbuilding, missiles, air defense, radars, satellites, and nuclear modernization inevitably results in multi-year lags to real-world scaling in Cold War environments. Overinsurance is better than underinsurance when dealing with asymmetric downside—defense budgets can be cut relatively quickly, but war zones cannot be redeveloped at such a pace.

On a global level, world military expenditures are hitting record highs, according to SIPRI. NATO also has allies committing to investing 5% of GDP annually by 2035.

The defense of Taiwan through arms is about increasing the probability that aggression fails quickly, or at least raising the expected duration and cost of conflict. That logic underpins the current discussion about significantly higher U.S. defense spending in 2027, as it reduces an attacker's confidence in achieving its mission.

Will we see a conflict over Taiwan? Maybe—defense stocks would likely rally in such an environment. However, it is not inevitable if deterrence holds through a combination of military signaling and diplomatic engagement. In my opinion, America will likely make it clear to China that any attempt to resolve the Taiwan issue by force would result in direct U.S. involvement. In either scenario, the defense spending upcycle is not abating, due to its roots in rational geopolitical risk control rather than political ideology.

AI Trade Unwind

I'm a medium-term investor and trader, so when I see that there has never been a moment in modern-era market history where the market has delivered a positive return when anywhere near the current deviation from the average, I'm not a buyer. This is the moment when the market gets tested, and commentators get sifted from true allocators. In other words, we are heading into the moment where consensus "breaks." Ironically, those who are humble enough to simply focus on the macro valuations and not strange notions and technical reasons for an infinite bull run will be the survivors. Others who cannot see with clarity will almost certainly fade into average returns.

The last time we were at a valuation like this was in 1999. I don't know what else to tell you—is this time different? I wouldn't bank on that. I'm 30% in cash and equivalents, and that will be rising over the next 6–9 months. In my humble opinion, this is the last year of the current bull market.

The above aggregate market value is derived from the following indicators, each of which I study in-depth regularly and which reveal almost no reason to be bullish as a medium- or long-term investor.

In fact, we have very pessimistic economic uncertainty, along with very pessimistic consumer confidence, as well as an extremely overvalued equity market. What we are looking at, ladies and gentlemen, are the cardinal signs of a bubble.

I would like to draw your attention closer to the following graphic:

Above 1.5 standard deviations higher than the modern-era market average, the five-year return on the S&P 500 is anywhere between -15% and -21%. In other words, to be frank, you are better off not buying stocks as a long-horizon investor at this juncture. Put simply: move to cash and cycle into low-beta, like defense stocks.

Examples of defense stocks in my current portfolio include RTX Corporation (RTX), Rheinmetall AG (RNMBY), BAE Systems plc (BAESY), and Rolls-Royce Holdings plc (RYCEY). These are all surging recently amid operations and narrative, but that is not the point of holding them. In an AI capex unwind, defense stocks will be the last chess pieces standing. Please note that in the following 2021–2022 chart, Rolls-Royce was undergoing structural difficulties, but it is now showing a resurgence with stronger leadership. Therefore, RR should perform better in an AI capex de-rating environment where major indices like the Nasdaq-100 and the S&P 500 fall aggressively.

Conclusion

Markets are natural organisms, and so keep in mind that there is little way to avoid a bubble bursting. What wise capital allocators do is cycle cash early into the next wave of structural resiliency in the future macro environment that will be emerging. There is no use in being hopeful other than in your private prayers, and there is no use in trying to convince yourself that this time is different—because physics, like an eternal law, does not change unless the constituent elements do. Ladies and gentlemen, the constituent elements are physically a bubble but also show a defense boom in response to Chinese geopolitical competition that could meaningfully—but not totally—offset the impending 2027 decline.
